# Atlantic Ocean present vs future, rcp85 scenario



## Maps

### U wind component

  <tr>
    <td style="text-align:center;vertical-align:top;width:33%">

      <figcaption>Fig.3: rcp85 CMIP5 ensemble U-wind component future-present difference 10-year average</figcaption>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

The general $W$-$E$ wind patterns are similar between present and future (Fig.1 & 2). In the future though, the westerlies in the  Southern Ocean become stronger around their southernmost boundary of 60$^oS$ (Fig.3), while the opposite happens around their divergence zone at 30$^oS$. At the same time, the trade winds become stronger close to 30$^oS$ but weaker around the equator. A similar change is seen in the Northern hemisphere around 30$^oN$ & 60$^oN$, only of lower magnitude. The trade winds seem to expand to higher latitudes as the divergence zones at 30$^o$ (white colour in Fig.1 & 2) show a negative change in the future, meaning stronger easterly winds. Simultaneously, the convergence zone at 60$^o$ (white colour in Fig.1 & 2) is moving to higheer latitudes.

  <tr>
    <td style="text-align:center;vertical-align:top;width:33%">

		<figcaption>
    Fig.4: rcp85 CMIP5 ensemble U-wind component 2011-20 std
    </figcaption>
    </td>
  <td style="text-align:center;vertical-align:top;width:33%">
    <img src="/Users/nomikosskyllas/Documents/projects/Maarten_project/Figures/20210512_fig5.png" alt="20210512_fig5" style="zoom:67%;" />
    <figcaption>
    Fig.5: rcp85 CMIP5 ensemble U-wind component 2086-95 std
    </figcaption>

    <img src="/Users/nomikosskyllas/Documents/projects/Maarten_project/Figures/20210512_fig6.png" alt="20210512_fig6" style="zoom:67%;" />
    <figcaption>
    Fig.6: rcp85 CMIP5 ensemble U-wind component future-present std difference 10-year mean
    </figcaption>
    </td>
  </tr>
</table>

The variability (standard deviation of daily values) of the U component is at its highest close to Greenland and South Africa with low values seen around the equator (Fig.4 & 5), meaning that the trade winds are the most consistent ones. In the future, variability decreases between 60$^oS$ and 60$^oN$ for the Atlantic Ocean but increases in the Arctic making the wind more unpredictable only in this region (Fig.6).

    </figcaption>

The general $N$-$S$ wind patterns are similar between present and future (Fig.7 & 8). In the future, the equatorward speed of the surface winds increases over 30$^oN$ and below 30$^oS$ (Fig.9). North winds in the Arctic and south winds in the Antarctic slow down, meaning that the polar cells' surface winds become weaker.

The variability (standard deviation of daily values) of the V component is at its highest in high latitudes, especially between Greenland and Norway, with low values seen around the equator (Fig.10 & 11). In the future, variability once again decreases between 60$^oS$ and 60$^oN$ for the Atlantic Ocean but increases in the Arctic making the wind more unpredictable only in this region (Fig.12).

The same pattern as in Fig.1 & 2 can be seen here too: Trade winds around the equator and the mid-latitude westerlies. In the future, the westerlies become stronger for both hemispheres at higher latitudes, around 60$^o$(Fig.15), while they slow down around 30$^o$. The trade winds expand to higher latitudes, while slowing down at the equator.

The variability (standard deviation of daily values) of the U component is at its highest in the Southern Ocean and at the coast of Greenland (Fig. 16 & 17), and it reaches its minimum values in the tropics. In the future, variability increases only in the Arctic and in the rest of the Atlantic Ocean either remains stable (tropics) or decreases (mid-latitudes).

The strong equatorward movement of the winds in the tropics is also seen in Fig.19 and 20 along with the weaker, mid-latitude poleward winds. The projected future change suggests that the equatorward winds become stronger at the Hadley cell edges, above 30$^oN$ and below 30$^oS$. The speed of the poleward winds increases at their edges as well, meaning above 60$^oN$ and below 60$^oS$,

The variability (standard deviation of daily values) of the V wind component (Fig.22 & 23) is similar to that of the U component (Fig. 16 & 17),  showing high values in the Southern Ocean and at 60$^oN$ with minimum values in the tropics. Similarly to the U component (Fig.21) In the future, variability increases only in the Arctic and in the rest of the Atlantic Ocean either remains stable (tropics) or decreases (mid-latitudes).

## A first conlcusion

According to the rcp85 scenario, between the years 2086-95 ("future") and 2011-20 ("present"), the two wind components show a maximum change in their mean wind speed values of approximately 18% (U) and 13%(V). Their projected variablity change maxima are 6% and 8% respectively. The largest mean wind speed changes are located around 30$^o$ and 60$^o$ for both hemispheres, suggesting changes at the boundaries of the atmospheric circulation cells. Both Hadley-Ferrel divergence zones and Ferrel-Polar convergence zones show a tendency for moving to higher latitudes. Winds become more unpredictable in the Arctic region, while their variability decreases at mid-latitudes and remains stable in the tropics.
